introduction.subtitle: Operating maturity assessment. 5 min.
introduction.description:
Operating maturity diagnostic for founder focus, decisions, metrics, and sustainability.

This educational diagnostic runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.duration: 8 diagnostic questions plus 4 evidence checks. Approximately 5 min.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How many company-level priorities are truly active this quarter?

options.text: A. No reliable practice exists yet; the answer depends on intuition or one-off effort.
B. There is an informal practice, but it depends on individuals and has weak repeatability.
C. The practice is documented, repeatable, and used in important decisions.
D. The practice is governed, evidence-backed, measured, and continuously improved.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How well does your calendar reflect the company highest-leverage work?

options.text: A. No reliable practice exists yet; the answer depends on intuition or one-off effort.
B. There is an informal practice, but it depends on individuals and has weak repeatability.
C. The practice is documented, repeatable, and used in important decisions.
D. The practice is governed, evidence-backed, measured, and continuously improved.
Not sure / not applicable.
A. Authority is unclear, improvised, or concentrated in one person.
B. The practice is informal; boundaries, escalation, or records are inconsistent.
C. Owners, decision rules, and review points are documented for important cases.
D. Authority, evidence standards, exception handling, and periodic review are governed.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: What happens after an important decision?

options.text: A. Authority is unclear, improvised, or concentrated in one person.
B. The practice is informal; boundaries, escalation, or records are inconsistent.
C. Owners, decision rules, and review points are documented for important cases.
D. Authority, evidence standards, exception handling, and periodic review are governed.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How are company goals connected to operating metrics?

options.text: A. The information is fragmented, missing, or too inconsistent to support decisions.
B. Partial tools or reports exist, but definitions and ownership are weak.
C. Definitions, owners, access rules, and regular review are governed.
D. Lineage, quality controls, exception handling, and a feedback loop into decisions exist.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How does the leadership team use metrics?

options.text: A. The information is fragmented, missing, or too inconsistent to support decisions.
B. Partial tools or reports exist, but definitions and ownership are weak.
C. Definitions, owners, access rules, and regular review are governed.
D. Lineage, quality controls, exception handling, and a feedback loop into decisions exist.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How stable are your energy, attention, and recovery?

options.text: A. The area is mostly unmapped, reactive, or dependent on informal trust.
B. The area is known in broad terms, but controls, owners, or evidence are incomplete.
C. Owners, controls, thresholds, and response or remediation paths are documented.
D. Controls are tested, monitored, reviewed after incidents or changes, and integrated into operating governance.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How much does the organisation depend on you personally?

options.text: A. No reliable practice exists yet; the answer depends on intuition or one-off effort.
B. There is an informal practice, but it depends on individuals and has weak repeatability.
C. The practice is documented, repeatable, and used in important decisions.
D. The practice is governed, evidence-backed, measured, and continuously improved.
Not sure / not applicable.

contextQuestions.text: What level of evidence supports your answers for Focus & Trade-offs?

options.text: Opinion: mostly judgement, memory, or individual perception.
Anecdotal: examples exist, but they are not systematically documented.
Documented: there are current notes, models, policies, metrics, or records.
System-verified: evidence is routinely produced by a governed workflow, system, or review cadence.
